title: Let's Make a Sandbox (Realm Fables, Solo RPG, tools, sandbox)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LNWGlnt3wF4

Daniel (@dungeondive) populates a few random hexes live on camera using *Realm Fables Volume 2*, the *Forbidden Lands* Legends & Adventurers booklet, and a standard deck of cards. The premise: drop d20s on a blank hex grid, draw a card for each, consult the Realm Fables world-map prompts, and let the rolls suggest landmarks. He rolls up a tomb of a dead god, a desert region, a plateau of waist-high weeds, hidden temples behind a magical border, a thieves' market, a fortress, a merchants' village with trade routes, a wooded region, and — crucially — a patch of "strange blue grass" that may or may not be alien.

Then he turns to the *Forbidden Lands* legend generator (Daniel's standout pick from an otherwise too-complex system) and rolls a 1,100-year-old tale of a cruel raven-sister who travelled to the dead god's land out of a promise and starved to death — a hook that immediately ties the blue grass, the dead god, and the ruined land together into one connected mystery. The video is part demonstration, part argument for keeping prep loose and letting tools surface the connections.

----

How much of a sandbox's flavour comes from the generators you pick versus your willingness to find narrative connections between unrelated rolls?
